content.text: in Python's asyncio is the primary mechanism for scheduling and running coroutines concurrently within a single thread. While a plain coroutine sits idle until it is explicitly awaited, a Task wraps that coroutine and schedules it on the event loop so it can run alongside other coroutines without blocking them.
function in Python, calling it does not execute its body — it returns a coroutine object. That coroutine does nothing until something awaits it. If you simply
a coroutine directly, execution pauses at that point and resumes only after the coroutine completes. There is no concurrency yet.

that takes a coroutine and submits it to the running event loop immediately. The event loop holds a queue of ready-to-run steps, and the Task registers itself there. This means the Task's coroutine starts running as soon as the event loop gets a free moment — not just when you call
on the Task object itself. You can create multiple Tasks before awaiting any of them, and the event loop will interleave their execution at every

import asyncio

async def fetch(name, delay):
    print(f"{name} started")
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)
    print(f"{name} done")
    return name

async def main():
    # Sequential — no concurrency. Total time ≈ 3 seconds.
    await fetch("A", 1)
    await fetch("B", 2)

    # Concurrent — Tasks scheduled immediately. Total time ≈ 2 seconds.
    task_a = asyncio.create_task(fetch("A", 1))
    task_b = asyncio.create_task(fetch("B", 2))
    await task_a
    await task_b
is cooperative multitasking: coroutines voluntarily yield control at
points so other work can proceed. But if every coroutine is awaited sequentially, you get no concurrency at all — each one must finish before the next begins. Tasks solve this by decoupling

. You schedule all the work upfront, then collect results. The event loop runs them concurrently, and total wall-clock time collapses to roughly the longest single task's duration rather than the sum of all durations.

This matters most in I/O-bound workloads: waiting for network responses, reading files, querying databases, calling APIs. While Task A waits for a server to reply, Task B can send its own request, and Task C can process a result that already arrived.
The event loop is a loop that repeatedly picks the next ready step and runs it until that step hits an
object is created, wrapping the coroutine.
The Task registers a callback with the event loop using
, which places it in the ready queue.
Control returns to your calling code immediately. The Task has not started yet.
On the next iteration of the event loop, the Task's step function calls
, advancing the coroutine to its first
or a socket read) is not yet done, the Task suspends and registers a callback to resume when that object completes.
The event loop moves on to the next ready Task or callback.
When the awaited I/O or timer finishes, its completion fires the callback, and the Task's coroutine resumes from where it paused.

This cycle repeats until the coroutine returns or raises an exception. The Task then stores the result or exception and marks itself done, which triggers any callbacks registered on the Task (including anything awaiting it).---
This is the standard way to create a Task in Python 3.7+:

import asyncio

async def worker(n):
    await asyncio.sleep(n)
    return f"result-{n}"

async def main():
    task = asyncio.create_task(worker(2), name="my-worker")
    result = await task
    print(result)  # result-2
requires a running event loop. Calling it at module level or from a synchronous context raises a
. It must be called from inside a coroutine or callback that is already running on the loop.
parameter assigns a human-readable label to the Task, useful for debugging. You can retrieve it later with
served the same purpose. It accepts either a coroutine or a Future/Task and schedules it. If passed a coroutine, it wraps it in a Task. If passed an existing Future or Task, it returns it unchanged.
in new code — it is more explicit and only accepts coroutines, which prevents subtle bugs from accidentally passing a non-coroutine.
If you have a reference to the loop itself, you can call
directly. This is useful in older code that manages its own event loop lifecycle explicitly.
, it exposes a standard set of state-querying methods. Understanding these is important for writing robust code that handles cancellation, errors, and result collection correctly.

task = asyncio.create_task(worker(2))

task.done()       # True if finished (result, exception, or cancelled)
task.cancelled()  # True if cancel() was called and succeeded
task.result()     # Returns result; raises if not done or if exception/cancelled
task.exception()  # Returns stored exception or None; raises if not done or cancelled
task.get_name()   # Returns the task's name string
task.get_coro()   # Returns the underlying coroutine object
A Task that raised an exception inside its coroutine is still
on it will re-raise that exception. This is how exceptions propagate from background tasks to the code that awaits them.
Awaiting a Task blocks the current coroutine until the Task finishes, then returns its result (or re-raises its exception):

async def main():
    task = asyncio.create_task(worker(1))
    # ... other work here runs concurrently with worker ...
    result = await task  # collect when ready
    print(result)
The key insight is that the Task is already running from the moment
is reached, the Task may already be done, and the
The most common pattern for running several Tasks at once and collecting all results:

async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        worker(1),
        worker(2),
        worker(3),
    )
    print(results)  # ['result-1', 'result-2', 'result-3']

accepts both coroutines and Task objects. When given coroutines, it wraps them in Tasks automatically. Results are returned in the same order as the input arguments, regardless of which Task completed first.
By default, if any Task raises an exception,
cancels the remaining Tasks and re-raises the exception. Set
to instead collect exceptions as regular return values:

results = await asyncio.gather(
    worker(1),
    broken_worker(),
    worker(3),
    return_exceptions=True,
)
# [result, ExceptionObject, result]
is an async context manager that collects Tasks, waits for all of them, and handles errors with a more structured pattern:

async def main():
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        task_a = tg.create_task(worker(1))
        task_b = tg.create_task(worker(2))
    # Both tasks are guaranteed done here
    print(task_a.result(), task_b.result())
If any Task within the group raises an exception, the group cancels all remaining Tasks and re-raises all exceptions together as an
because it is impossible to forget to await the tasks — the context manager handles it.
accepts a set of awaitables and returns two sets: done tasks and pending tasks. It gives fine-grained control over which completion event to react to:
— return as soon as any Task finishes or is cancelled.
— return as soon as any Task raises (or all complete successfully).
Yields results as each Task finishes, in completion order rather than creation order. Useful when you want to process results immediately as they arrive:
Cancellation is a first-class feature of Tasks. Calling
to be raised at the Task's next

async def long_job():
    try:
        await asyncio.sleep(100)
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        print("Cleaning up...")
        raise  # ALWAYS re-raise CancelledError

async def main():
    task = asyncio.create_task(long_job())
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    task.cancel()
    try:
        await task
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        print("Task was cancelled")

after cleanup. Swallowing it leaves the Task in an indeterminate state and breaks structured concurrency patterns. Cleanup work (releasing locks, closing files, rolling back state) should happen in the
message, useful for debugging which code triggered the cancellation.
to protect an inner awaitable from being cancelled when the outer Task is cancelled:

Tasks support adding done callbacks, which fire when the Task completes (for any reason — success, exception, or cancellation):
Callbacks are always called synchronously in the event loop thread when the Task transitions to done. They are not coroutines — they are plain functions.
to inspect every live Task in the current event loop, and
to get the Task that is currently executing:

async def worker():
    me = asyncio.current_task()
    print(f"I am {me.get_name()}")

async def main():
    tasks = asyncio.all_tasks()
    print(f"Running tasks: {[t.get_name() for t in tasks]}")
Architecture: Event Loop and Task Interleaving
This illustrates the core benefit: three Tasks with combined durations of 5.5 seconds finish in 3.5 seconds of wall time because their I/O wait periods overlap.
When to Use Tasks (and When Not To)
Multiple I/O-bound operations can run concurrently: fetching several URLs, querying databases, reading multiple files.
You want to start background work while continuing other processing.
You need to implement timeouts, retries, or cancellation over concurrent operations.
You want to limit concurrency through a semaphore controlling how many Tasks run at once.
The work is CPU-bound (calculations, compression, image processing). Because asyncio is single-threaded, a Task doing heavy computation blocks the entire event loop. Use
You need true OS-level thread parallelism. asyncio gives
You are in a synchronous context. You cannot create or await Tasks outside a running event loop.
The following shows a realistic pattern for fetching multiple URLs concurrently, limiting concurrency with a semaphore, and handling both results and errors cleanly:

import asyncio
import aiohttp

async def fetch_url(session, url, sem):
    async with sem:  # max 10 concurrent requests
        try:
            async with session.get(url, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=5)) as resp:
                text = await resp.text()
                return {"url": url, "status": resp.status, "length": len(text)}
        except Exception as e:
            return {"url": url, "error": str(e)}

async def main():
    urls = [
        "https://example.com",
        "https://httpbin.org/delay/1",
        "https://httpbin.org/status/404",
        # ... hundreds more
    ]
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10)  # never more than 10 at once

    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [asyncio.create_task(fetch_url(session, url, sem)) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

    for r in results:
        print(r)

asyncio.run(main())
All Tasks are created before any are awaited, maximising concurrent scheduling.
means a single failing URL does not abort all others.
is shared across all Tasks — it manages connection pooling internally.

Creating a Task without ever awaiting it means the result (including exceptions) is silently discarded. Python 3.8+ emits a warning for Tasks whose exceptions were never retrieved, but it is easy to miss. Always await Tasks or add a done callback.
in Python 3.8+ — note: in 3.7 it was
or be explicit about which exceptions you handle.

Blocking the event loop with synchronous code.
, making synchronous database calls, or doing CPU-heavy work inside a coroutine blocks every other Task. Use
for blocking I/O or CPU work.
Creating tasks in a tight loop without any yielding.
If you create thousands of Tasks in a loop body with no

anywhere, all are created before any can start. This is usually fine, but if each Task immediately needs a resource (like a semaphore slot), the coordination overhead can spike. Structure the loop to yield periodically with
A Task that is created but not stored in a variable can be garbage-collected while still running, which silently terminates it. Always keep a reference — either
it, store it in a collection, or use

# WRONG — may be GC'd
asyncio.create_task(background_job())

# CORRECT — store reference
task = asyncio.create_task(background_job())
background_tasks.add(task)
task.add_done_callback(background_tasks.discard)
for structured concurrency whenever possible. It enforces that all spawned Tasks complete before the block exits and provides cleaner error propagation than
output readable and traceback messages more informative.
Always handle exceptions from Tasks. If you use
, an exception in one Task cancels all others. If you use
, check each result for exception instances. Neither approach is automatically "safe" — the right choice depends on whether partial results are acceptable.
to enforce time limits on Tasks:
creates a fresh event loop, runs the coroutine to completion, and tears the loop down cleanly. It should be the single entry point for top-level async code.
A single asyncio event loop can manage tens of thousands of concurrent Tasks, because suspended Tasks consume very little — just the memory for their coroutine frame and the stored state at each
. Unlike OS threads, there is no stack allocated per Task and no context-switch overhead from the kernel.

Throughput scales with I/O concurrency, not CPU count. Adding more Tasks beyond what the network or disk can service simultaneously provides no additional benefit. The practical limits are usually the remote service's rate limits, connection pool sizes, or memory for coroutine frames when dealing with millions of simultaneous Tasks.
For CPU-bound parallelism alongside asyncio, the canonical pattern is using
. This offloads the blocking call to a separate process and returns an awaitable that the event loop can wait on without blocking.
wraps a coroutine and schedules it on the event loop immediately, enabling concurrent execution without threads.
is the standard way to create Tasks; it requires a running event loop.
are the main tools for managing multiple Tasks and collecting results.
Tasks are suitable for I/O-bound concurrency; CPU-bound work requires
Always hold a reference to a Task to prevent silent garbage collection while it is running.
is the preferred modern pattern for structured concurrency, replacing many uses of bare
